name: Incline Dumbbell Row
instructions: Set an incline bench to roughly 30-45 degrees and lie chest-down with a dumbbell in each hand, arms hanging straight.
Brace your core and keep your chest pinned to the pad — the chest support is the whole point.
Drive your elbows up and back, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top.
Lower the dumbbells under control until your arms are fully extended.
Avoid lifting your chest off the bench or jerking the weight up with your hips.

name: Kroc Row
instructions: Stand next to a sturdy support, hinge at the hips, and brace one hand on the support — torso roughly parallel to the floor.
Grip a heavy dumbbell with the free hand, letting it hang straight down with a neutral grip; use a strap if grip is the limiter.
Drive the elbow up and back toward the hip in a powerful arc, allowing some controlled body english.
Lower under control to a full stretch, then explode the next rep — the goal is high reps with a heavy load.
Keep the lower back neutral; cheating from the hips is fine, but don't round your spine.

name: Bird Dog Row
instructions: Get on all fours, then extend the opposite leg straight back so your body forms a stable bird-dog plank.
Hold a dumbbell in the working hand with the arm hanging straight below the shoulder.
Without rotating your torso, row the dumbbell to your ribs — squeeze shoulder blade and pause briefly.
Lower under control and resist the urge to twist; the anti-rotation challenge is the point.
Keep hips square to the floor; if you wobble, drop the weight before adding load.

name: Renegade Row
instructions: Start in a high plank with hands gripping two hex/flat-sided dumbbells directly under your shoulders, feet wide for stability.
Perform a strict push-up, keeping hips level and core braced — no piking, no sagging.
At the top, row one dumbbell to your ribs while the opposite arm presses hard into the floor.
Lower the dumbbell, then perform another push-up and row with the other arm — that's one rep.
Avoid hip rotation; widening the feet helps anti-rotation if you're new to the movement.

name: Elbows-Out Bent Row
instructions: Hinge at the hips with a neutral spine until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor, dumbbells hanging with a pronated (overhand) grip.
Row the dumbbells up and out — elbows flare wide to roughly 90 degrees from your torso.
Drive elbows toward the ceiling; squeeze upper back and rear delts at the top.
Lower under control, keeping the torso angle stable — don't stand up between reps.
Use a lighter load than a standard bent row; the wide elbow path biases upper back over lats.

name: Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in the hand opposite the standing leg; the other leg hovers off the floor.
Hinge at the hip, letting the free leg extend straight back as a counterweight, keeping a soft bend in the standing knee.
Lower the dumbbell along the standing shin until you feel a deep hamstring stretch — torso and trailing leg form one straight line.
Drive the standing-leg heel into the floor and squeeze the glute to return to standing.
Avoid letting the hips open — point the trailing-leg toes down to keep the pelvis square.

name: B-Stance Romanian Deadlift
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width, then slide one foot back so only the toes are down (kickstand) — about 60-70% of the load goes to the front leg.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand in front of your thighs; brace the core and set a neutral spine.
Hinge at the hips, pushing them back, lowering the dumbbells along the front shin until you feel a strong hamstring stretch.
Drive through the front-foot heel and squeeze the front glute to stand back up.
Use the kickstand foot only for balance — don't push off it.

name: Dumbbell Hip Thrust
instructions: Sit on the floor with your upper back against a sturdy bench, knees bent, feet flat and roughly shoulder-width.
Place a dumbbell across the crease of your hips — a pad or folded towel makes this far more comfortable.
Drive through your heels and extend the hips up until the torso and thighs form a straight line; squeeze glutes hard at the top.
Lower under control without letting the hips fully rest on the floor between reps.
Keep the chin tucked and ribs down; don't hyperextend the lower back at lockout.

name: Dumbbell Glute Bridge
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent, feet flat and hip-width apart, heels close to your glutes.
Set a dumbbell across the crease of your hips — hands lightly steady it.
Drive through your heels and extend the hips toward the ceiling; squeeze the glutes hard at lockout.
Lower under control until the hips just brush the floor, then go again.
Keep the ribs down — don't arch the lower back to fake more range.

name: Bulgarian Split Squat
instructions: Stand a long stride in front of a bench; place the top of one foot on the bench behind you.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides; chest tall, core braced.
Lower the back knee toward the floor by bending the front knee — front shin stays close to vertical for a quad bias, or lean forward slightly for more glute.
Drive through the front-foot heel and mid-foot to return to the top.
Don't push off the rear foot — it's only for balance. Finish all reps on one side before switching.

name: Goblet Squat
instructions: Hold a single dumbbell vertically against your chest, cupping the top end with both hands — elbows tucked under it.
Stand with feet shoulder-width, toes slightly out; brace the core.
Squat down between your legs, knees tracking over toes, until elbows brush the inside of your knees or you hit comfortable depth.
Drive through mid-foot to stand back up; keep the chest tall throughout.
Avoid letting the dumbbell pull you forward — keep it pinned tight to your chest.

name: Deficit Reverse Lunge
instructions: Stand on a sturdy plate or low step (2-4 inches) with a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Step one foot back and down off the platform, lowering the rear knee toward the floor in a controlled lunge.
Front foot stays planted on the deficit — the elevation gives you extra range and a deeper glute/quad stretch.
Drive through the front-foot heel to step back up onto the platform.
Alternate legs or finish all reps on one side; keep the torso upright and ribs stacked.

name: Seated Single-Leg Calf Raise
instructions: Sit on a bench with the ball of one foot on a small block or plate so the heel can drop below toe level.
Rest a dumbbell vertically on top of the working knee, steadying it with one hand.
Let the heel drop fully for a deep stretch in the calf.
Drive the ball of the foot down to push the heel as high as possible; pause and squeeze.
Lower under control — quick bouncy reps cheat the soleus out of work.

name: Squeeze Press
instructions: Lie on the floor (or a bench if available) with a dumbbell in each hand, neutral grip — palms facing each other.
Press the dumbbells together hard so the inner edges touch and stay in contact through every rep.
Press straight up, maintaining the squeeze — pecs should be screaming.
Lower the dumbbells under control to your chest, never breaking the squeeze.
Use lighter weight than a standard DB bench; the constant adduction is the stimulus, not the load.

name: Floor Press
instructions: Lie on your back on the floor with knees bent or legs extended, a dumbbell in each hand at chest level.
Press the dumbbells straight up to lockout, palms facing forward (or neutral if shoulders prefer).
Lower under control until your triceps touch the floor; pause briefly — the floor caps your range and protects the shoulders.
Press back up explosively without bouncing the elbows.
Keep the lower back neutral — no excessive arching.

name: Single-Arm Floor Press
instructions: Lie on your back on the floor with a dumbbell in one hand at chest level; the free arm rests out for balance or holds the floor.
Brace the core hard — anti-rotation is half the work in a single-arm press.
Press the dumbbell straight up to lockout without rotating the torso.
Lower until the triceps touches the floor, pause, then press up again.
Finish all reps on one side before switching; keep hips and shoulders square throughout.

name: Dumbbell Cuban Rotation
instructions: Stand tall with a light dumbbell in each hand, arms hanging in front with a pronated grip.
Perform an upright row, lifting the elbows up and out until upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor — the elbows lead.
Keeping the elbows fixed, externally rotate the forearms up and back until they point toward the ceiling.
Reverse the rotation back to elbow-high, then lower the dumbbells back to the start.
Use very light weight — this is rotator-cuff prehab, not a load lift.

name: Arnold Press
instructions: Stand tall with a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height, palms facing your face (supinated), elbows tucked in front.
Press the dumbbells overhead while rotating the wrists outward — finishing with palms forward at lockout.
Lower by reversing the path — rotate palms back toward your face as the dumbbells come down to the front of the shoulders.
Keep the core braced and ribs down; don't let the lower back arch to cheat the press.
Use lighter load than a standard overhead press — the rotation eats into your top-end strength.

name: Seated Arnold Press
instructions: Sit on an upright bench with back support; dumbbells at shoulder height, palms facing you, elbows in front.
Press the dumbbells overhead while rotating the wrists outward — palms finish facing forward at lockout.
Lower under control, reversing the rotation back to the supinated start position.
Press your back into the bench and keep the core tight — no lower-back arch.
Seated removes lower-body cheating, so expect to use less weight than the standing version.

name: Farmer Walk
instructions: Stand between two heavy dumbbells, hinge to grip them, then stand up tall with chest proud and shoulders pulled back.
Brace the core and walk forward in short, controlled steps — don't waddle or let the dumbbells swing.
Keep the ribs stacked over the pelvis — no leaning to one side.
Walk for time or distance; common targets are 30-60 seconds or 30-50 yards per set.
Set the weights down with control — don't drop them from full height.

name: Hindu Push-Up
instructions: Start in a downward-dog position — hips high, hands and feet on the floor, arms and legs straight, butt pointing up.
Bend the elbows and sweep the chest down and forward in an arc, like diving under a low fence.
Continue the wave by extending the arms as your hips drop low and your chest comes up — finishing in an upward-dog position.
Reverse the motion or push back to downward-dog to start the next rep.
Move smoothly — jerky transitions hammer the lower back. Keep the core engaged throughout.

name: Archer Push-Up
instructions: Start in a wide-hand push-up position, hands well outside the shoulders.
Lower toward one hand by bending that elbow while the opposite arm stays nearly straight — fingers of the straight arm can rotate outward.
The straight arm acts as a brace; almost all the load goes through the bending arm.
Press back up to the top, then lower toward the other side on the next rep.
Keep hips level — no piking. If too hard, do them on the knees first.

name: Pike Push-Up
instructions: Start in a push-up position, then walk your feet in toward your hands so your hips pike up high — body forms an inverted-V.
Hands shoulder-width, head positioned between the arms; the more vertical the torso, the more this loads the shoulders.
Bend the elbows to lower the crown of your head toward the floor between your hands.
Press back up to fully extended arms.
Avoid bending at the hips on the way up — keep the pike angle constant. Elevate the feet on a bench for a harder variation.

name: Dumbbell Skullcrusher
instructions: Lie back on a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand, pressed straight overhead with palms facing each other.
Keep your elbows fixed in space — only the forearms move.
Bend at the elbows and lower the dumbbells toward the sides of your head, just past your ears.
Drive back to the start by extending the elbows; squeeze the triceps at lockout without flaring the elbows out.
If the dumbbells start drifting back over the chest you've turned this into a press — keep elbows stacked over shoulders.

name: Single-Arm Skullcrusher
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with a dumbbell in one hand pressed straight up; the free hand can support the working elbow lightly.
Lock the elbow in place pointing toward the ceiling.
Lower the dumbbell across your body toward the opposite shoulder — this slight cross-body angle hits the long head harder.
Extend back up under control and squeeze.
Keep reps strict; ego loads turn this into a shoulder injury.

name: Tricep Kickback
instructions: Hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor, holding a dumbbell in each hand with elbows pinned tight to your ribcage.
Start with the elbows bent ~90 degrees and the dumbbells hanging down.
Without moving the upper arm, extend the forearms back until the arms are fully straight.
Pause at lockout and squeeze the triceps for a beat.
Lower under control. Picking weights light enough to avoid the upper arm swinging is the whole game.

name: Seated Dumbbell French Press
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support, holding one dumbbell vertically with both hands cupping the inner plate, pressed overhead.
Keep upper arms vertical and elbows pointing forward, close to your ears.
Bend at the elbows and lower the dumbbell behind your head until you feel a strong stretch in the triceps.
Drive back up to lockout without letting the elbows flare wide.
The behind-the-head position emphasises the long head — use moderate weight, the leverage is brutal.

name: Spider Curl
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 45 degrees and lie chest-down with arms hanging straight off the top of the pad, dumbbell in each hand.
The pad blocks your upper arms from swinging — that's the whole point.
Curl the dumbbells up by flexing only at the elbows, supinating the wrist as you rise.
Squeeze hard at the top, with the biceps fully shortened.
Lower under control to a full stretch. Strict form here builds the peak.

name: Pinwheel Curl
instructions: Stand with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing your body (neutral / hammer grip).
Curl one dumbbell up and across your body, finishing with the dumbbell near the opposite shoulder.
Keep the wrist neutral throughout — no rotation. The cross-body path biases the brachialis.
Lower under control and alternate arms.
Treat it as a strict, slow movement; the brachialis grows on time-under-tension, not speed.

name: Dumbbell Drag Curl
instructions: Stand holding dumbbells at your sides with a supinated grip.
Instead of curling forward, drag the elbows back as you flex, keeping the dumbbells close to your torso the entire range.
The dumbbells travel almost vertically up your sides rather than arcing in front of you.
Stop at chest level and squeeze — the front delt stays out of it.
Lower under control. Expect to use lighter weight than a strict curl; the leverage is harder.

name: Dumbbell Pullover
instructions: Lie crosswise across a flat bench so only your upper back is supported, hips low; hold one dumbbell vertically with both hands cupping the top plate.
Press it straight up over your chest with a slight bend in the elbows.
Reach the dumbbell back over your head in a wide arc, keeping the elbow bend constant — feel the stretch through your lats and chest.
Pull back to the start by squeezing the lats and chest together, not by extending the elbows.
Don't let the hips rise to compensate; the bench pressure should stay on your upper back the whole rep.

name: Dumbbell Push Press
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, dumbbells at shoulder height, palms facing forward.
Dip slightly by bending the knees — about a quarter-squat — keeping the torso upright.
Drive explosively through the legs and use that momentum to press the dumbbells overhead to lockout.
Lower under control back to the shoulders, reset, and repeat.
The leg drive is what lets you load heavier than a strict press; keep the core braced or the back will arch.

name: Dumbbell Thruster
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width, dumbbells at shoulder height, palms facing each other.
Squat down to at least parallel keeping the torso upright and elbows up.
Drive up out of the squat and immediately ride the momentum into a strict overhead press.
Lock the dumbbells out at the top; lower back to the shoulders as you descend into the next rep.
It's one continuous fluid movement, not a squat plus a press; pace yourself, this is conditioning as much as strength.

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Press
instructions: Lie back on a flat bench holding a single dumbbell in one hand pressed straight up; the free hand can rest on your thigh or the bench for stability.
Brace hard — the offset load wants to twist your torso. Keep your hips and shoulders pinned to the bench.
Lower the dumbbell to the side of the chest under control.
Press straight up to lockout without letting the bench tilt.
The anti-rotation demand makes the core do as much work as the chest. Use moderate weight at first.

name: Decline Push-Up
instructions: Set up in a push-up position with your feet elevated on a bench and hands on the floor, body straight from heels to head.
The elevated feet shift the load toward the upper chest and front delts.
Lower your chest to the floor with elbows tracking back at ~45 degrees.
Press back up to lockout, keeping the core braced so the hips don't sag.
If a full set feels easy, add a slow eccentric (3-second descent) before adding weight via a vest or plate.

name: Diamond Push-Up
instructions: Set up in a push-up position with your hands close together under your chest, thumbs and index fingers touching to form a diamond.
Keep your body straight and core braced; elbows should track back along your ribs, not flare wide.
Lower your chest to your hands, keeping the diamond shape stable.
Press back up to a strong lockout, squeezing the triceps.
The narrow hand position emphasises the triceps; if your wrists complain, widen the hands slightly to a close-grip push-up.

name: Cossack Squat
instructions: Stand with a wide stance, toes pointed slightly out, holding a dumbbell vertically against your chest in goblet position.
Shift your weight onto one leg and squat down to that side, keeping the bent knee tracking over the toes.
The trailing leg stays straight with the toes turned up — you should feel a deep stretch through the inner thigh.
Drive back up to standing and shift to the other side.
Move slowly; the bottom position requires hip mobility. Build depth gradually rather than forcing range.

name: Dumbbell Step-Up
instructions: Stand in front of a sturdy bench with a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Place one foot fully on the bench. Drive through that heel to stand all the way up onto the bench.
Avoid pushing off with the trailing foot — the working leg should do all the work.
Step back down under control with the trailing leg, then the working leg.
Complete all reps on one side before switching, or alternate. Choose a bench height that lets you start with the working knee at ~90 degrees.

name: Dumbbell Walking Lunge
instructions: Stand tall holding dumbbells at your sides.
Step forward with one leg into a lunge — front knee tracks over the toes, back knee descends toward the floor.
Drive through the front heel to stand and bring the back leg through into the next forward lunge.
Keep the torso upright and avoid letting the back knee crash into the floor.
Walk in a straight line for the prescribed reps per side. Reset if balance breaks down — quality over distance.

name: Dumbbell Lateral Lunge
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart holding dumbbells at the sides or one at the chest.
Take a wide step out to one side and sit your hips back into that side, bending the loaded knee while the trailing leg stays straight.
Aim for the working thigh roughly parallel to the floor; the trailing foot stays flat with toes forward.
Drive off the working leg to push back to standing.
Alternate sides or complete reps per side. The frontal-plane work is brutal for hip stability; expect humbling weights.

name: Single-Arm Romanian Deadlift
instructions: Stand tall holding a single dumbbell in one hand at your side; feet hip-width apart, knees soft.
Hinge forward at the hips with a flat back, letting the dumbbell drop straight down close to your shin.
Reach back with the hips — the rib cage and dumbbell stay over the toes; do not round the lower back.
Hinge until the hamstring of the working side reaches a strong stretch (around mid-shin for most people).
Drive the hips forward to stand tall, squeezing the glute. The single-arm load makes this an anti-rotation drill — resist the twist.

name: Dumbbell Sumo Squat
instructions: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width, toes pointed out about 30 degrees. Hold one dumbbell vertically by the top plate, hanging in front of your hips.
Squat straight down by sitting between your hips, knees tracking over the toes.
Aim for thighs parallel or below; chest up, lower back neutral.
Drive back up by pushing the floor apart with the feet — this fires the adductors and glutes.
If knees cave inward, narrow the stance or lighten the load.

name: Single-Leg Dumbbell Hip Thrust
instructions: Sit on the floor with your upper back against a sturdy bench, one foot flat on the floor, the other extended out straight.
Place a dumbbell over your hip crease, holding it steady with both hands.
Drive through the planted heel to lift your hips until your torso is parallel to the floor and your shin is roughly vertical.
Squeeze the glute hard at the top, ribs down, no lumbar over-extension.
Lower under control. The unilateral load exposes glute weakness; use lighter weight than a two-leg version.

name: Glute March
instructions: Set up in a hip-thrust position: upper back on a bench, dumbbell across the hips, both feet flat on the floor.
Lift the hips into a full thrust position — torso parallel to the floor, glutes squeezed.
Holding the bridge, march one knee up toward your chest while the other leg supports.
Lower the marching foot back to the floor and switch sides without letting your hips drop.
The challenge is keeping the hips level under the offset load — single-leg glute and core work without ever lowering.

name: Hollow Body Hold
instructions: Lie on your back with arms extended overhead and legs straight.
Press your lower back firmly into the floor — no daylight under the lumbar curve.
Lift the shoulders, head, and legs off the floor simultaneously to form a shallow banana shape.
Hold the position with the lower back glued to the floor. Breathe.
If your back arches, pull the legs up higher or bend at the knees until you can keep the back pinned. Time is the load — start with 20 s sets.

name: Dumbbell Plank Pull-Through
instructions: Set up in a high plank with hands under shoulders and feet wider than hip-width for stability. Place a moderate dumbbell just outside one hand.
Reach across with the opposite hand, grab the dumbbell, and drag it under your torso to the other side.
Reset to plank, switch hands, drag back. Keep the hips square — if they twist, the load is too heavy.
The core is fighting rotation the entire set; that's the point.
Pace each rep deliberately. Quality of the anti-rotation hold beats speed.
